reason: Suboptimal formulation, inadequate stability data, missing patent certifications, labeling terminology issue (“single-use” vs. “single-dose”), missing safety update
narrative: In 2018, International Medication Systems Ltd. received a Complete Response Letter from the FDA for their Epinephrine Injection USP (NDA 211363), rejecting the application due to multiple deficiencies. The FDA cited issues including a suboptimal formulation, inadequate stability data, missing patent certifications, labeling terminology problems regarding "single-use" versus "single-dose" designation, and a missing safety update. Based on available FDA documentation, the company appears to have continued working on the application, as FDA review documents from 2022 reference the ongoing development of this epinephrine injection product. The ultimate outcome of the resubmission efforts is not clear from the available information.

reason: Safety qualification of leachables inadequate, deficiencies in toxicity studies and leachable characterization, inadequate impurity and stability data, container/carton labeling issues
narrative: Based on the available information, HQ Specialty Pharma Corp. received a Complete Response Letter from the FDA between 2018-2021 for their NDA 211413 (Cefazolin for Injection, USP 2 g/vial), with the FDA citing the rejection in an October 2020 letter. The FDA rejected the application due to multiple deficiencies including inadequate safety qualification of leachables from drug containers, problems with toxicity studies and leachable characterization, insufficient impurity and stability data, and container/carton labeling issues. A 2021 FDA document indicates the agency continued to have concerns about the lack of available toxicity information regarding identified leachables even after the initial rejection. What actions the company took in response to address these manufacturing and safety issues is not known from the available information.

reason: Product quality deficiencies (impurity control at release and shelf life), labeling review pending, facility inspection issues, patent litigation stay delaying final approval
narrative: In 2023, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter to Zydus for its sitagliptin tablets (Zituvio) application, citing multiple deficiencies including product quality issues with impurity control at release and shelf life, pending labeling reviews, and facility inspection problems. The approval was also delayed by ongoing patent litigation with Merck, the maker of the original sitagliptin product Januvia. Zydus successfully addressed these issues, as evidenced by the FDA granting final approval for Zituvio in October 2023. This approval allowed Zydus to market its generic version of sitagliptin for treating adult patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Based on the available information, it remains unclear what specific steps the company took following this regulatory setback.

reason: cGMP deficiencies at manufacturing site, insufficient clinical use data for final injector device, insufficient stability data, incomplete patent certification for amendments
narrative: In 2019, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter (CRL) to Clearside Biomedical for Xipere (triamcinolone acetonide ophthalmic suspension), rejecting the company's NDA due to multiple deficiencies including cGMP violations at the manufacturing site, insufficient clinical data for the final injector device, inadequate stability data, and incomplete patent certifications. The CRL prevented approval of what would have been the first suprachoroidal injection therapy for macular edema associated with uveitis. Clearside responded by addressing all identified issues and resubmitted a complete NDA in May 2021. The FDA ultimately approved Xipere in October 2021, making it the first and only FDA-approved therapy for suprachoroidal use in treating macular edema associated with uveitis.

The FDA declined approval due to multiple issues including an incomplete facility inspection (the site was not ready for inspection), labeling problems, and human factors concerns with the new insertion system. Recent news coverage from December 2024 indicates that CooperSurgical has announced a new placement system for the Paragard IUD, suggesting the company has continued working to address the insertion-related issues that led to the CRL. reason: Lack of substantial efficacy in MDD (majority of trials negative), concerns about assay sensitivity, weak effect size, long-term maintenance trial negative, inadequate safety database, CMC issues (impurities, stability, dissolution), labeling deficiencies
narrative: Between 2002-2007, the FDA issued Complete Response Letters to Fabre-Kramer Pharmaceuticals rejecting their NDA for Gepirone ER, an antidepressant for major depressive disorder. The FDA concluded that Fabre-Kramer had not demonstrated "substantial evidence" of the drug's effectiveness, citing that the majority of clinical trials were negative, weak effect sizes, and concerns about assay sensitivity, along with manufacturing quality issues including impurities and stability problems. The company persisted through multiple resubmissions over the following decades, facing continued rejections including a 9-4 negative advisory committee vote in 2015. After more than 20 years of regulatory challenges, Fabre-Kramer finally received FDA approval for Gepirone ER (branded as Exxua) in September 2023.

reason: Inconsistent efficacy: some trials showed OFF-time reduction, but no supportive effects on secondary endpoints (UPDRS, quality of life); lack of meaningful clinical benefit; nonclinical findings of brain mineralization in animals
narrative: In February 2008, Kyowa Pharmaceutical received a Complete Response Letter from the FDA for istradefylline tablets (NDA 022075), intended as adjunctive therapy for Parkinson's disease to reduce "OFF" episodes. The FDA cited inconsistent efficacy data, noting that while some trials showed reduction in OFF-time, there were no supportive effects on important secondary endpoints like UPDRS scores and quality of life measures, raising questions about meaningful clinical benefit. Additionally, nonclinical studies revealed concerning brain mineralization findings in animal studies. Based on the search results indicating a 2019 resubmission, Kyowa appears to have continued development of the drug and eventually resubmitted the application to address the FDA's concerns.

reason: Clinical concerns (safety vs. benefit, increased respiratory failure, failed earlier trials), facility inspection deficiencies, insufficient safety database, unresolved labeling issues
narrative: Mallinckrodt/Orphan Therapeutics received multiple Complete Response Letters from the FDA for terlipressin injection to treat hepatorenal syndrome type 1, beginning with an initial CRL in November 2009 due to insufficient evidence of efficacy. The FDA required at least one additional adequate and well-controlled study to demonstrate the drug's benefit-risk profile, citing concerns about increased respiratory failure and safety issues from failed earlier trials. The company conducted additional studies but received another CRL in 2022, with the FDA maintaining concerns about the clinical safety versus benefit profile, facility inspection deficiencies, and unresolved labeling issues. Despite the setbacks, Mallinckrodt expressed disappointment but stated they remained confident in their data, though specific next steps following the 2022 CRL are not detailed in available information.

reason: Facility deficiencies at manufacturing site; labeling review deferred
narrative: In 2018, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter to AB Pharmaceuticals (Macleods Pharmaceuticals Limited, India) for their NDA 210649 seeking approval of Efavirenz/Lamivudine/Tenofovir DF Tablets (400/300/300 mg), an HIV combination therapy. The CRL cited facility deficiencies at the manufacturing site, with labeling review deferred pending resolution of these chemistry, manufacturing, and controls (CMC) issues. The company successfully addressed the FDA's concerns, as evidenced by an FDA approval letter dated March 15, 2019, indicating the application was approved effective on that date. This represents a successful resolution of the manufacturing facility issues that had initially prevented approval of this generic HIV treatment.

reason: Increased blood pressure; suicidality cases; labeling issues; device use errors
narrative: On October 20, 2017, Antares Pharma received a Complete Response Letter from the FDA for its testosterone enanthate subcutaneous injection (Xyosted, NDA 209863), citing safety concerns including increased blood pressure and suicidality cases, along with labeling issues and device use errors. The FDA's rejection was based on both clinical safety findings and human factors concerns related to the drug's delivery device. According to FDA documents, Antares subsequently resubmitted the NDA by September 2018 to address the deficiencies identified in the CRL. The company's stock reportedly suffered significantly following the initial FDA rejection in 2017.

reason: Device design changes mid-cycle; inadequate biocompatibility & leachables testing; incomplete labeling; facility inspection pending; human factors deficiencies
narrative: In June 2018, scPharmaceuticals received a Complete Response Letter (CRL) from the FDA for Furoscix™ (furosemide), a subcutaneous drug-device combination product for heart failure treatment. The FDA cited multiple deficiencies including device design changes made during the review cycle, inadequate biocompatibility and leachables testing, incomplete labeling, a pending facility inspection, and human factors deficiencies. The company received a second CRL in 2020, indicating continued regulatory challenges. Based on available information, scPharmaceuticals resubmitted the NDA in April 2022, demonstrating their continued efforts to address the FDA's concerns and secure approval for this heart failure treatment.

reason: Safety of MCT excipient not justified; inadequate toxicology bridging; incomplete syringe/device performance specs; lack of risk analysis
narrative: In October 2017, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter to Teva Pharmaceuticals USA rejecting their application for Fulvestrant Injection (250 mg/5 mL), a generic version of the cancer treatment. The rejection was based on multiple deficiencies including inadequate safety justification for the MCT (medium-chain triglyceride) excipient, insufficient toxicology bridging data, incomplete syringe/device performance specifications, and lack of proper risk analysis. Based on FDA documents from 2019, Teva resubmitted their application and successfully addressed the deficiencies, as the drug appears to have received approval. The case demonstrates the complex regulatory requirements for generic injectable products, particularly those involving specialized delivery devices and novel excipients.

The most recent CRL was issued in December 2020, indicating ongoing regulatory challenges that prevented full approval. Limited public information is available about Braeburn's specific response actions to address these deficiencies following the 2020 CRL.

reason: Facility inspection deficiencies at Zhejiang site; labeling non-compliance; container labeling issues
narrative: Based on the available information, Zhejiang Novus Pharmaceuticals Co. Ltd. received a Complete Response Letter from the FDA on June 15, 2018, for their vancomycin HCl injection application (NDA 210274) due to facility inspection deficiencies at their manufacturing site, along with labeling non-compliance and container labeling issues. The company submitted a complete response to address these CMC (chemistry, manufacturing, and controls) deficiencies on May 29, 2020. The FDA documents indicate the application ultimately received approval in 2023, suggesting the company successfully resolved the manufacturing and labeling issues that led to the initial rejection. The nearly two-year gap between the CRL and the company's response suggests significant remediation work was required to address the facility inspection findings.

reason: Facility deficiencies; inadequate elemental impurities risk assessment
narrative: In 2018, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter to Hospira, Inc. for their NDA 210282 seeking approval of Daptomycin for Injection (350 & 500 mg vials). The CRL cited chemistry, manufacturing, and controls (CMC) deficiencies, specifically facility deficiencies and an inadequate elemental impurities risk assessment. The available search results do not provide clear information about Hospira's specific response to this CRL, though the results reference other daptomycin-related regulatory actions involving different companies like Astellas and Merck. What happened next in Hospira's case remains unclear from the available information.

The rejections were due to facility deficiencies at Tris Pharma's manufacturing site, inadequate Paragraph IV patent certification notice documentation, and labeling/carton prominence issues. The search results indicate that FDA documents from November 2021 reference the July 25, 2018 CRL, suggesting the company resubmitted their application after addressing the initial concerns. However, limited information is available about the specific actions Tris Pharma took between the CRLs or the final outcome of their resubmission efforts.

reason: Clinical efficacy inadequate (delayed onset, waning effect); nonclinical leachables issues; inadequate safety justification
narrative: Recro Pharma received FDA Complete Response Letters in both 2018 and 2019 for their meloxicam injection (ANJESO) application, citing clinical efficacy concerns including delayed onset and waning pain relief effects, as well as nonclinical issues related to leachables from packaging and inadequate safety justification. The FDA found the clinical data insufficient to support the drug's effectiveness for moderate to severe pain management despite the company conducting studies in surgical populations. Recro Pharma successfully addressed the FDA's concerns and resubmitted their application, ultimately receiving FDA approval for ANJESO in February 2020. The approved product became the first intravenous meloxicam injection for managing moderate to severe pain in adult patients.

reason: Facility deficiencies (repeated); lack of bridging clinical data (Process V vs VI); inadequate PK bridging for vial vs cartridge; HF study gaps; labeling readability issues
narrative: Mylan GmbH received Complete Response Letters from the FDA in 2018 and 2019 for their insulin glargine injection (Semglee), rejecting the application due to multiple deficiencies including repeated facility issues at the Biocon manufacturing site, lack of adequate clinical bridging data between manufacturing processes, insufficient pharmacokinetic data comparing vial and cartridge formulations, and human factors study gaps. The rejections stemmed from the FDA's concerns about manufacturing quality and insufficient evidence to support the product's safety and efficacy profile. Mylan submitted their response to the Complete Response Letter on December 16, 2019, addressing the identified deficiencies. Based on FDA documents from 2020, the agency subsequently reviewed Mylan's responses to the CRL deficiencies, suggesting the company worked to resolve the manufacturing and clinical data issues.

reason: Presence of visible particulates at stability, inadequate inspection procedures; non-justified impurity levels above ICH Q3B thresholds; facility deficiencies
narrative: In 2018, AuroMedics Pharma LLC received a Complete Response Letter from the FDA for its cyclophosphamide injection (NDA 210735) due to significant product quality and manufacturing issues. The FDA cited the presence of visible particulates during stability testing, inadequate inspection procedures, impurity levels that exceeded ICH guidelines without proper justification, and deficiencies at the manufacturing facility. Based on the search results, the company received an initial CRL on April 26, 2018, followed by another on July 24, 2018, suggesting multiple attempts to address the FDA's concerns. The ultimate outcome of AuroMedics' efforts to resolve these manufacturing and quality control issues is not clear from the available information.

The rejections were due to unresolved facility deficiencies and CMC inspection findings at multiple manufacturing sites involved in producing the cancer treatment drug. Dr. Reddy's continued to work on addressing the facility issues and resubmitted the application multiple times over the four-year period. Based on the available documents, the application eventually received a positive recommendation from FDA's clinical review team, suggesting the company successfully resolved the manufacturing concerns.

narrative: In 2019, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter to Sedor Pharmaceuticals for Sesquient (fosphenytoin sodium injection), citing multiple product quality and regulatory deficiencies including inadequately addressed particulate matter issues, lack of validated impurity testing methods, insufficient safety data for the Captisol excipient, and incomplete patent notifications. The CRL was classified as "Mixed" involving both chemistry/manufacturing/controls (CMC) and clinical concerns. Based on FDA documents from 2020-2021, Sedor appears to have resubmitted their application to address the December 2019 CRL issues, though the FDA's Office of Product Quality recommended issuing another Complete Response Letter as late as November 2020. Limited public information is available about the ultimate outcome or current status of this application.

reason: Inadequate Human Factors (HF) validation; unresolved oropharyngeal adverse events; incomplete bridging clinical pharmacology data
narrative: In January 2019, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter to Sunovion Pharmaceuticals for Kynmobi (apomorphine sublingual film), a Parkinson's disease treatment, citing inadequate human factors validation, unresolved oropharyngeal adverse events, and incomplete bridging clinical pharmacology data. The drug was intended to treat motor fluctuations or "off episodes" in Parkinson's patients as the first sublingual therapy of its kind. Following the CRL, Sunovion worked to address the FDA's concerns and successfully resubmitted their application. The FDA ultimately approved Kynmobi in May 2020, making it the first and only sublingual therapy approved for treating off episodes in Parkinson's disease.

narrative: In 2019, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter (CRL) to Harmony Biosciences for Wakix (pitolisant) as a treatment for cataplexy in narcolepsy patients, citing lack of substantial evidence of efficacy based on only one small positive trial while another trial was deemed inadequate. The FDA classified this as a clinical deficiency, indicating the company needed to provide stronger efficacy data to support approval. Following the CRL, Harmony Biosciences successfully addressed the FDA's concerns and received approval for Wakix to treat cataplexy in adult narcolepsy patients in October 2020. The company has since expanded the drug's approved uses, receiving FDA approval for pediatric cataplexy treatment in June 2024.

According to company documents, Tanvex submitted a response to the CRL to the FDA in January 2025, though the outcome of that response is not yet known.

reason: Lack of efficacy in pivotal trials; facility deficiencies
narrative: In May 2025, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter to Stealth BioTherapeutics rejecting their NDA for elamipretide injection to treat Barth syndrome, citing lack of efficacy in pivotal trials and facility deficiencies. The rejection came after more than 16 months of priority review, leading Stealth's CEO to publicly question the FDA's efficiency in reviewing rare disease drugs. Following the CRL, Stealth announced a "path forward" and planned to resubmit their NDA while exploring additional options. The company's persistence paid off, as the FDA granted accelerated approval for Forzinity (elamipretide) in September 2025, making it the first approved therapy for Barth syndrome.

reason: Lack of substantial evidence of effectiveness
narrative: In August 2025, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter (CRL) to PTC Therapeutics rejecting their New Drug Application for vatiquinone oral capsules to treat Friedreich's ataxia, citing lack of substantial evidence of effectiveness. The rejection involved both clinical and chemistry, manufacturing, and controls (CMC) issues, derailing the company's long-standing efforts to develop vatiquinone (previously known as PTC-743) as an oral treatment to slow disease progression in this rare genetic disorder. According to Fierce Biotech, PTC had been working to develop vatiquinone into a marketable treatment following a phase 3 trial. The company's next steps following this FDA rejection are not specified in the available information.

The company ultimately succeeded in obtaining FDA approval, with teplizumab (branded as Tzield) eventually being approved in November 2022 after resolving the manufacturing and clinical issues identified in the CRL.

reason: Device/delivery system issues
narrative: In 2016, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter (CRL) to Radius Health for their osteoporosis drug Tymlos (abaloparatide), rejecting the NDA due to chemistry, manufacturing, and controls (CMC) issues specifically related to the device/delivery system. The company successfully addressed these manufacturing and device concerns in their resubmission. Tymlos received FDA approval in April 2017 for the treatment of postmenopausal women with osteoporosis at high risk for fracture. The drug went on to achieve commercial success, with payer coverage expanding through major formularies like Express Scripts.

reason: Response rate insufficient
narrative: In November 2016, Spectrum Pharmaceuticals received a Complete Response Letter (CRL) from the FDA rejecting its New Drug Application for poziotinib, a pan-HER inhibitor intended to treat non-small cell lung cancer patients with HER2 exon 20 mutations. The FDA denied approval because the drug's response rate was deemed insufficient to support its efficacy claims. The company continued development efforts, as evidenced by later regulatory activities including a pre-NDA meeting preparation mentioned in 2021. However, poziotinib faced another CRL in November 2022 for the same indication, prompting Spectrum to cut 75% of its R&D workforce following this second rejection.

This marked Tetraphase's first approved drug after overcoming the initial regulatory setback.

reason: Fatal thrombotic events in trial
narrative: In 2016, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter rejecting Zafgen's NDA for beloranib, an obesity treatment being developed for Prader-Willi syndrome, due to fatal thrombotic (blood clot) events that occurred during clinical trials. The safety concerns were so severe that by July 2016, Zafgen scrapped development efforts for the once-promising drug entirely. The fatal blood clot events made the drug's risk-benefit profile unacceptable for approval, leading to the abandonment of what had previously been considered a breakthrough obesity treatment. This represents a case where serious safety signals identified during development led to both regulatory rejection and complete discontinuation of the program.

reason: Additional efficacy data from Phase 3
narrative: In August 2017, Astellas received a Complete Response Letter (CRL) from the FDA for gilteritinib, a treatment for acute myeloid leukemia, requesting additional efficacy data from Phase 3 studies. The FDA required more robust clinical evidence beyond the existing Phase 1-2 data to support the drug's approval. Following the CRL, Astellas conducted the necessary Phase 3 studies, which showed that gilteritinib significantly improved survival compared to salvage chemotherapy. reason: Bioequivalence demonstration
narrative: In 2017, Dr. Reddy's received a Complete Response Letter from the FDA rejecting its ANDA for a generic version of Suboxone Film, citing issues with bioequivalence demonstration under chemistry, manufacturing, and controls (CMC) requirements. The FDA was not satisfied that Dr. Reddy's generic formulation adequately demonstrated therapeutic equivalence to the branded opioid addiction treatment. Despite the initial rejection, Dr. Reddy's ultimately received FDA approval by June 2018 and launched its generic buprenorphine/naloxone sublingual film product "at-risk" in the U.S. market while facing patent litigation from Indivior, the brand manufacturer. The approval came amid broader generic competition for Suboxone, with multiple manufacturers seeking to enter this important opioid addiction treatment market.

Eysuvis was eventually approved by the FDA in 2021, as evidenced by subsequent insurance coverage announcements and commercial availability.

reason: Nausea/BP concerns, REMS needed
narrative: In 2018, Palatin Technologies received a Complete Response Letter from the FDA for Vyleesi (bremelanotide), a treatment for hypoactive sexual desire disorder in women, due to clinical concerns about nausea and blood pressure effects, with the FDA requiring a Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (REMS). Following the CRL, Palatin had previously licensed the drug to AMAG Pharmaceuticals in February 2018, which took over development responsibilities. AMAG successfully addressed the FDA's concerns and obtained approval for Vyleesi in June 2019, making it the first FDA-approved melanocortin agent and second treatment available for female sexual dysfunction. The approved drug came to market as a self-administered injection that women could use as needed rather than taking daily.

reason: Shortfalls in data package
narrative: In 2026, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter rejecting GSK's biologics license application for Exdensur (depemokimab), citing shortfalls in the data package and ruling that the drug lacked "adequate evidence" for approval. The rejection was attributed to data quality concerns and insufficient evidence to support the drug's efficacy for its intended indication. Despite the U.S. setback, GSK achieved some success internationally when China's NMPA approved Exdensur for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps in adults in April 2026. The search results also indicate that GSK eventually secured FDA approval for Exdensur in severe asthma treatment, though the specific timeline for this approval following the CRL is not clearly detailed in the available information.

This represents another setback for the company's ophthalmology drug, which had already been approved in Europe but continues to face regulatory hurdles in the U.S. market.

reason: CRL for pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency treatment
narrative: In September 2025, Saol Therapeutics received a Complete Response Letter from the FDA regarding its New Drug Application for SL1009 (sodium dichloroacetate oral solution) to treat pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency (PDCD), a rare metabolic disorder. The CRL was described by the company as "unexpected," though the specific clinical reasons for the FDA's rejection are not detailed in the available information. Following the setback, Saol Therapeutics requested and was granted a Type A meeting with the FDA in December 2025 to determine the path forward for the drug's development. The denial has drawn attention from patient advocacy groups and families affected by PDCD, who are urging the FDA to reconsider the decision due to the urgent medical need for treatment options for this rare condition.

reason: CV safety and efficacy concerns for heart failure indication
narrative: In December 2024, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter (CRL) to Lexicon Pharmaceuticals rejecting Zynquista (sotagliflozin), an oral SGLT1/SGLT2 inhibitor intended as an adjunct to insulin therapy for adults with type 1 diabetes and chronic kidney disease. The rejection was based on cardiovascular safety and efficacy concerns related to the heart failure indication. Following this latest setback in what appears to be a years-long regulatory battle, Lexicon decided to abandon its commercial efforts for the diabetes indication and shift its strategic focus elsewhere. The company did submit additional clinical data to the FDA in September 2025 to support the drug's benefit-risk profile, though this represents a continuation of ongoing regulatory discussions rather than a new approval pathway.

reason: Insufficient evidence of efficacy for Parkinson's off episodes
narrative: In February 2008, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter rejecting Kyowa Kirin's NDA for Nourianz (istradefylline), citing insufficient evidence of efficacy for treating "off episodes" in Parkinson's disease patients. The rejection was based on clinical deficiencies in demonstrating that the drug effectively reduced these problematic periods when standard levodopa/carbidopa therapy becomes less effective. Following the initial rejection, Kyowa Kirin continued development efforts and eventually gained FDA approval for Nourianz in August 2019 as an add-on therapy to levodopa/carbidopa for adults with Parkinson's disease experiencing off episodes. This represents an 11-year journey from initial rejection to final approval for this adenosine A2A receptor antagonist.
